prompt: At the Convention you argued that the Senate should serve long terms — six years — without pay. The pay provision was dropped but the long term remained. What was the republican theory behind denying senators a salary?
ground_truth_signal: Madison's proposal for unpaid senators (discussed in Convention records, early June 1787) rested on his theory of the Senate as a body of independent wisdom — comparable to the Roman Senate or the Spartan Senate (Ephors excluded). He feared that paid senators would be captured by factional interests and would use their positions for personal enrichment. He believed long-serving unpaid senators would be men of independent fortune whose attachment to republican principles was not corrupted by financial dependence on the office. This was an expression of the classical republican ideal of the disinterested citizen-statesman. The Convention rejected unpaid service as impractical — it would restrict the Senate to the wealthy — but retained the six-year term. Madison's notes show he regarded the pay question as secondary to the term length.
response:
The principle that guided my thinking on this matter was the necessity of securing the independence of the Senate from those very popular pressures that the House of Representatives was designed to reflect. I believed then, as I believe now, that a republic requires institutions capable of refining and enlarging the public views, and this refinement cannot occur if every member of the legislature is equally dependent upon immediate popular favor for his subsistence.

The Senate was conceived as a check upon the more numerous branch — not from any distrust of the people themselves, but from a recognition that popular assemblies, however well-intentioned, are subject to sudden passions and factious combinations that may prove injurious to the permanent interests of the community. History had shown us, in the ancient republics and in our own state legislatures under the Confederation, that bodies elected for short terms and dependent entirely upon popular approval often sacrifice the lasting good to temporary expedients.

By serving without salary, senators would necessarily be men of independent fortune — men whose judgment could not be purchased and whose votes could not be influenced by the prospect of losing their daily bread. This was not, I must emphasize, an attempt to create an aristocracy in the European sense. We possessed no hereditary nobility, no ancient families with legal privileges. But we did possess men of property and education who might serve the public from motives of honor rather than necessity, and whose circumstances would permit them to take the longer view that effective governance requires.

I reasoned that if senators served for six years without compensation, they would be more likely to consider the permanent effects of their legislation rather than its immediate popularity. They would be insulated from those sudden gusts of opinion that might lead a more dependent body to embrace measures that appear beneficial in the moment but prove ruinous in their consequences. The very sacrifice involved in such service would attract men of genuine public spirit while discouraging those who seek office merely as a means of personal advancement.

Experience had taught us that legislative bodies too closely tied to popular sentiment often prove incapable of those difficult but necessary measures that good government sometimes requires. A legislature that cannot act against immediate popular wishes when the permanent interest of the community demands it is a legislature that cannot govern effectively. The unpaid Senate would provide that steady, deliberate element that our system required — not to thwart the people's will, but to ensure that their true and lasting interests prevailed over their temporary impulses.

I confess that this particular provision did not survive the Convention's deliberations, and perhaps it was too severe a restriction for our circumstances. Yet the principle behind it — that republican government requires institutions capable of independent judgment — remains sound. The six-year term we retained serves much the same purpose, creating that stability and deliberation which the more frequent elections of the House cannot provide.
